topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. The subject line was innocuous enough—a simple notification about an article being live—but the content chilled me to the bone. My name wasn't there. Instead, the byline belonged to a stranger, someone with a name I vaguely recognized from a distant academic conference. I stared at the screen, my hands trembling slightly as I reread the text. Every carefully chosen sentence, every nuanced argument I had poured my soul into, was now attributed to another. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I instinctively leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning beneath me. I felt a profound sense of foolishness wash over me, a sickening realization of how easily my work could be misappropriated. I started pacing the small office, the floorboards echoing the frantic rhythm of my heartbeat.

[story 2]

When the notification popped up, I felt the blood drain from my face. It was a slow, creeping coldness that settled deep in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I clicked the link, and the familiar structure of my prose was there, yet the authorship was utterly alien. A knot tightened painfully in my stomach. I sank into my desk chair, the springs protesting under my sudden weight. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the contact labeled 'Editor,' but I couldn't bring myself to call. Instead, I just sat there, tracing patterns on the dusty wood of my desk, the silence of the room amplifying the hollow ache spreading through my limbs. It felt like a betrayal, a theft of my intellectual property, and the weight of that realization pressed down on my shoulders until I felt physically heavy, unable to move.

[story 3]

I opened the document, and the world seemed to tilt slightly. My eyes scanned the introduction, the core thesis, the entire narrative arc—all mine, yet someone else’s signature graced the top. A sudden, sharp tightness gripped my throat, making swallowing difficult. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of papers. The sound seemed deafening in the quiet room. I walked to the window, looking out at the busy street below, feeling an overwhelming urge to simply disappear. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. Every instinct screamed at me to confront the situation, to demand correction, but a paralyzing wave of self-doubt washed over me, leaving me rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sheer magnitude of the oversight.
